headline: Sharp-Billed Shorebirds Return to East Coast Beaches
meta_description: American Oystercatchers have increased 45% since 2008 thanks to nonprofit efforts protecting their beach nesting sites from disturbance.
summary_short: Once hunted nearly to extinction, American Oystercatchers are thriving again along the East Coast thanks to $10 million in conservation work.
body:
The American Oystercatcher's distinctive orange bill and alert gaze are becoming a more familiar sight along North American shores. The shorebird's population has grown by 45 percent since 2008—a remarkable recovery for a species that was nearly wiped out at the turn of the 20th century.

Hunting and egg collecting devastated these birds a century ago. But a concerted conservation effort has changed their trajectory. Several nonprofits pooled resources to raise $10 million, directing funds toward one critical goal: protecting the birds' vulnerable beach nesting sites.

The work is painstaking and specific. During breeding season, conservationists rope off beaches to keep human activity away. Vehicle traffic restrictions further shield nesting areas from disturbance. At places like Trustom Pond National Wildlife Refuge in Rhode Island, wildlife monitors work directly with the birds, tracking their progress and ensuring protection measures are working.

This recovery demonstrates how targeted, science-based conservation can reverse even steep declines. The oystercatcher's comeback is not accidental—it required dedication, funding, and a clear understanding of what these birds need to thrive. For the first time in over a century, their future looks genuinely promising.
